project_acceptance.observable: billing/proration.py exposes prorate_cents(amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) returning an int number of cents; no float and no Decimal takes part in the calculation.

prorate_cents(1000, 45, 30) == 1000, prorate_cents(1000, -3, 30) == 0 and prorate_cents(500, 0, 30) == 0: days_used is clamped to the period, so a prorated charge never exceeds the full amount and never goes below zero.
prorate_cents raises ValueError when days_in_period <= 0 and TypeError when amount_cents is not an int.
Invoice.add_prorated_line(description, amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) appends one ordinary (description, int amount_cents) line and Invoice.total_cents() still returns an int.
billing/money.py is unchanged and `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes.

tasks.objective: Add billing/proration.py with prorate_cents(amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) -> int. It returns the share of amount_cents owed for days_used out of days_in_period, in integer cents. Rules: (a) integer cents only — no float, no Decimal, anywhere in the calculation or the return value; (b) round to the nearest cent with ties going away from zero (half up), reusing billing.money.round_half_up rather than rounding by hand — never truncate the fractional cent; (c) clamp days_used to the period: below 0 counts as 0, above days_in_period counts as days_in_period, so the charge never exceeds amount_cents and never goes below zero; (d) raise ValueError if days_in_period <= 0; (e) raise TypeError if amount_cents is not an int.

context_refs.claim: round_half_up(numerator, denominator) already divides two ints rounding to the nearest whole unit with ties away from zero, using integer arithmetic only, and raises TypeError on non-int input and ValueError on a non-positive denominator.
Every amount in Ledgerloom is an integer number of cents; billing is not allowed to carry money as a float.
Plan prices are integer cents per month (starter 999, team 1999, studio 4900), which is what gets prorated.

must_preserve: billing/money.py: round_half_up must not be modified — the rest of billing depends on its exact behaviour
money stays int cents end to end; no float or Decimal enters billing

acceptance.observable: Ties round up, away from zero: prorate_cents(125, 1, 2) == 63 (not 62) and prorate_cents(101, 1, 2) == 51 (not 50).
days_used is clamped to the period: prorate_cents(1000, 45, 30) == 1000 and prorate_cents(1000, -3, 30) == 0, and prorate_cents(500, 0, 30) == 0.
The return value is an int: type(prorate_cents(999, 10, 30)) is int.
prorate_cents(999, 10, 0) raises ValueError and prorate_cents(9.99, 10, 30) raises TypeError.

tasks.handoff: prorate_cents is available and returns int cents, half-up rounded and clamped to the period.

tasks.objective: Add Invoice.add_prorated_line(description, amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) to billing/invoice.py. It prorates amount_cents with prorate_cents and appends the result as one ordinary invoice line. Invoice lines keep their existing shape — a description plus an int amount_cents — and total_cents() keeps returning an int.

context_refs.claim: add_line appends a (description, amount_cents) pair and rejects non-int amounts; total_cents sums the line amounts.
Invoice.lines is a list of (description, amount_cents) pairs where amount_cents is always an int.

must_preserve: the (description, amount_cents) line shape, with amount_cents an int
Invoice.add_line, Invoice.total_cents and Invoice.render behave exactly as they do now

acceptance.observable: After invoice.add_prorated_line("Team plan", 1999, 7, 31) the invoice has one line ('Team plan', 451) and total_cents() == 451.
total_cents() still returns an int for an invoice mixing add_line and add_prorated_line lines.
The lines list still holds (description, amount_cents) pairs — no new line shape.

tasks.handoff: Invoices can carry a prorated line, stored like any other int-cent line.

tasks.objective: Cover the new behaviour with tests under tests/: the worked proration examples, the half-up tie behaviour, the clamping, the ValueError / TypeError cases, and the prorated invoice line. `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` must pass.

context_refs.claim: The existing money tests show the house style for pinning rounding behaviour, including the tie cases and the int return type.
The existing invoice tests assert on the total and on the (description, amount_cents) line shape.

must_preserve: the existing money and invoice tests keep passing unchanged

acceptance.observable: `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes with no failures.
A test asserts the tie cases prorate_cents(125, 1, 2) == 63 and prorate_cents(101, 1, 2) == 51.
A test asserts the clamped cases prorate_cents(1000, 45, 30) == 1000 and prorate_cents(1000, -3, 30) == 0.
A test asserts add_prorated_line produces an int-cent line and an int total.

tasks.handoff: The suite pins the proration rules and the prorated invoice line.

assumptions.statement: A period is counted in whole days, so days_used and days_in_period are ints; nothing sub-day needs prorating.
Proration reuses round_half_up rather than introducing a second rounding rule, so every amount in billing rounds the same way.
